title: Vivek Gupta vs Vice Chairman on 5 March, 2007
body: Vivek Gupta vs Vice Chairman on 5 March, 2007 -1- IN THE COURT OF SMT. PINKI ADDITIONAL DISTRICT JUDGE : DELHI. auauauauauauauauauauauauauauauauauauaua RCA NO. - 21/06 DATE OF INSTITUTION : 29/4/06 DATE OF DECISION : 05/3/07 IN THE MATTER OF :- D D DD D D D D D D D D VIVEK GUPTA, S/O SH. TILAK RAJ, R/O MUKTESHWAR DHAMA COMPLEX, ADNHERIA MODE, MEHRAULI, NEW DELHI-30. .....APPELLANT VERSUS VICE CHAIRMAN, DELHI DEVELOPMENT AUTHORITY, VIKAS SADAN, NEAR INA COLONY, NEW DELHI. .....RESPONDENT Contd..... -2- ORDER F F F F F F F 1. Vide this order, I shall propose to decide the following four applications, which have been moved by appellant/applicant/plaintiff :- (a) The first application is dated 26/4/2006, moved u/s 5 of the Limitation Act r/w Sec.151 CPC on 28/4/2006, for condonation of delay in filing the ''plaint'' dismissed on the ground of limitation. (b) The second application is dated 26/4/2006, moved on 28/4/2006, u/o XLI R-3 A r/w Sec.151 CPC for condonation of delay in filing ''appeal''. (c) The third application is u/o XLI R-23 R/w Sec. 107 & 151 CPC, dated 26/4/2006, moved on 28/4/2006, for remanding back the suit for fresh trial. (d) The fourth application is dated 26/4/2006, moved on Contd..... -3- 28/4/2006, u/o XLI R-27 r/w Sec. 107 & 151 CPC, for production of additional evidence. 2. I have heard Sh. Yog Verdhan, Advocate, Ld. counsel for appellant/applicant/plaintiff and Sh. K.D. Sharma, Advocate, Ld. counsel for respondent/Non- applicant/DDA and carefully perused the record as well as the impugned order. I have given my considerable thought to the submissions put forth by Ld. counsel for parties. 3. It is pertinent to mention that the case was already fixed for orders and substantial portion of the order was already dictated when written arguments dated 01/3/2007 have been filed by Sh. Yogvardhan, Advocate, Ld. counsel for appellant/applicant on 03/3/2007. I have carefully considered these written arguments also. 4. The present appellant/applicant has filed a suit through his power of attorney Sh. Sadashiv Gupta. This suit has been dismissed by the Ld. Civil Judge, vide his order dated 11/11/2005. The suit has been dismissed by way of passing Contd..... -4- detailed judgment. While deciding issue no.1, the Ld. Trial Court had dealt with point of limitation and sec. 3 of Limitation Act, 1963. As per Ld. Trial Court, the relief prayed by appellant/applicant/plaintiff were time barred. It has also been observed in para 15 of the impugned judgment in following words :- ''Following the mandate of Section 3 , Limitation Act, 1963, as interpreted by the Hon'bleSupreme Court in the aforesaid case, this court has no option but to decline the relief prayed by the plaintiff which are now time barred.'' 5. The other grounds considered by Ld. Trial Judge are regarding attorney of plaintiff. In para 16, 17 & 18 of the impugned judgment the Ld. Trial Judge had dealt with, on the point of attorney in the following words and came to the conclusion that the attorney cannot be allowed to represent plaintiff. Para 16 ''I have still more reasons to say that the plaintiff is not entitled for the relief of mandatory injunction as prayed in the suit. As already observed in the the opening Contd..... -5- paragraph of this judgment, Sh. Sadashiv Gupta has signed and verified this plaint mentioning himself as ''plaintiff''. In the amended memo of parties, Sh. Sadashiv Gupta has been mentioned as Power of Attorney of the plaintiff only and Sh. Vivek Gupta is shown as the plaintiff. In para 1 of the plaint, again Sh. Sadashiv is referred as plaintiff and it is written that ''The plaintiff is the attorney of Sh. Vivek Gupta, S/o Sh. Tilak Raj, R/o Mukteshwar Complex, Andheria Mod, New Delhi-110030 as such he is authorised signatory and is filing the case on his behalf.'' Thus, Sh. Sadashiv Gupta is referring himself as attorney of the plaintiff in the memo of parties but in the body of the plaint he is referring himself as the plaintiff. The business, if any, was being run by Sh. Vivek Gupta and in my opinion, of there is any entitlement of alternative allotment, the same could have been only in favour of Sh. Vivek Gupta and not in favour of anybody else. The attorney of the plaintiff cannot get the allotment in his favour representing himself as the plaintiff. In the absence of any claim or retification by Sh. Vivek Gupta, the prayer in the suit is not maintainable on behalf of Sh. Sadashiv Gupta treating himself as the original plaintiff.'' Para 17 ''Even if it is assumed that the plaint has filed by Sh. Sadashiv Gupta is maintainable, I am of the opinion, that on the basis of the evidence led on his behalf, no relief as prayed in the suit can be granted. I am of the considered opinion that in the present case, the plaintiff cannot be held entitled for the alternative allotment on the basis of the witnesses examined in the suit. Sh. Contd..... -6- Sadashiv Gupta who is the star witness on behalf of the plaintiff claims himself to be the attorney of Sh. Vivek Gupta who was running the alleged business whose structure was demolished by the defendant authority. The attorney has not stated in his examination that he himself was running the business on behalf of the plaintiff. He has sought to prove some documents originals of which have not been produced on the record. The documents being various bills and Income Tax Receipts are marked by the attorney. The originals of these documents were not available with the attorney. In the whole examination, the attorney is referring to the plaintiff and stating some facts on behalf of the plaintiff. The facts stated in the examination in chief of Sh. Sadashiv Gupta related to the personal knowledge of the person who was running the business and whose premises was demolished i.e. the plaintiff Sh. Vivek Gupta in the said case.'' Para 18 ''Theevidence of the attorney cannot substitute that of the plaintiff. In the facts and circumstances of the present case, I am of the opinion that best evidence could have been that of the plaintiff Sh. Vivek Gupta himself and he could not have been substituted by Sh. Sadashiv Gupta, because the facts regarding the running of the business over the suit premises, could have been better disclosed by him. By non-production of Sh. Vivek Gupta, the defendant is deprived of his valuable right to cross-examine a competent witness. The cross-examination of Sh. Sadashiv Gupta also reflects that he was not aware of the complete facts under dispute in the suit. He has Contd..... -7- expressed his unawareness about any correspondence made by Sh. Vivek Gupta in connection with his business. In his cross- examination, he also stated that to the best of his knowledge, Sh. Vivek Gupta was running a business in the name and style of M/s. Shri Marble Craft & Emporium, since 1980. This cross-examination is sufficient to reflect that he is not sure about the running of the business by Sh. Vivek Gupta in particular name and with effect from particular period. '' 6. Section 85 of Indian Evidence Act 1872 : Presumption as to power -of -attorney: The Court shall presume that every document purporting to be a power -of-attorney, and to have been executed before and authenticated by, a Notary Public or any Court, Judge, Magistrate, (Indian) Consul or Vice Consul, or representative of the Central Government was so executed and Authenticated. Section 101 of Indian Evidence Act 1872: Burden of proof : Whoever desires any court to give judgment as to any legal right or liability dependent on the existence of facts which he asserts, must prove that those facts exist. When a person is bound to prove the existence of any fact, it is said that the burden of proof lies Contd..... -8- on that person. Order VI Rule 14 Code of Civil Procedure ,1908 Pleading to be signed: Every pleading shall be signed by the party and his pleader (if any): Provided that where a party pleading is , by reason of absence or for other good cause, unable to sign the pleading, it may be signed by any person duly authorized by him to sign the same or to sue or defend on his behalf. 7. The Apex Court in the following judgement has discussed regarding power of attorney. In Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani and another Vs. Indusind Bank Ltd. , AIR 2005, Supreme Court, 439, Honble Mr. Justice D.M. Dharmadhikari & Hon'ble Mr. Justice H.K. Sema vide para 13 of this judement held that : ''Order III Rules 1 & 2 , CPC , empowers the holder of power of attorney to 'act'on behalf of the principal. In our view the word 'acts'employed in Order III, Rules 1 and 2 CPC ,confines only in respect of 'acts' done by the power of attorney holder in exercise of power granted by the instrument. The term 'acts'would not include deposing in place and instead of the principal. In other words , if the power of attorney holder has rendered some 'acts' in pursuance to power of attorney, he may depose for the principal in respect of such acts, but he cannot depose for the Contd..... -9- principal for the acts done by the principal and not by him. Similarly, he cannot depose for the principal in respect of the matter which only the principal can have a personal knowledge and in respect of which the principal is entitled to be cross-examined.'' 8. Vide para 15 of this judgement it has been discussed that :- ''Apart from what has been stated, this Court in the case of Vidhyadhr Vs. Manikrao and another , (1999) 3 SCC 573 observed at page 583 SCC that where a party to the suit does not appear in the witness box and states his own case on oath and does not, offer himself to be cross-examind by the other side, a presumption would arise that the case set up by him is not correct.'' 9. Vide para 17 it has been observed that :- ''On the question of power of attorney the High Courts have divergent views. In the case of Shambu Dutt Shastri Vs. State of Rajsthan 1986 (2) WLL 713, it was held :- that a general power of attorney holder can appear, plead and act on behalf of the party but he cannot become a witness on behalf of the party. He can only appear in his own capacity. No one can delegate the power to appear in witness box on behalf of himself 'To appear' in a witness box is altogether a Contd..... -10- different act. A general power of attorney holder cannot be allowed to appear as a witness on behalf of the plaintiff in the capacity of the plaintiff.'' 10. Vide para 18 of this judgment it was held that:- ''The aforesaid judgment was quoted with the approval in the case of Ram Prasad Vs. Hari Narain & Ors. AIR 1998 Raj. 185. It was held that the word 'acts' used in Rule 2 of Order III of the CPC does not include the act of power of attorney holder to appear as a witness on behalf of a party. Power of attorney holder of a party can appear only as a witness in his personal capacity and whatever knowledge he has about the case he can state on oath but he cannot appear as a witness on behalf of the party in the capacity of that party. If the plaintiff is unable to appear in the court, a Commissioner for recording his evidence may be issued under the relevant provisions of the CPC .'' 11. Vide para 21 of this judgment, it was held that :- ''We hold that the view taken by the Rajasthan High Court in the case of Shambhu Dutt Shastri (Supra) followed and reiterated in the case of Ram Prasad (Supra) is the correct view. The view taken in the case of Floriano Armando Lut's 2002(2) Bombay CR 754 cannot be said to have laid down a correct law and is accordingly overruled.'' Contd..... -11- 12. Our own High Court has discussed regarding Section 85 of Indian Evidence Act 1872 with regard to presumption as to power of attorney. 13. Hon'ble Mr. Justice Arun Kumar Judge, Delhi High Court (As his Lordship then was) and Hon'ble Mr. Justice Manmohan Sarin Judge, Delhi High Court dealt with similar situation wherein power of attorney was executed on 17.7.1981 and notarial certificate is dated 18.07.1981. 14. Vide para 8 of the judgement titled Birla D/w Ltd Vs. Prem Engineeing Works 1999 I AD (Delhi )379, it has been observed that :- ''The power of attorney was routinely tendered in evidence and exhibited. The question that comes up for consideration is whether a presumption of its due execution and validity can be raised under Section 85 of the Indian Evidence Act.'' A Division Bench of this Court had occasion to consider this aspect in Electric Construction & Equipment Co. Ltd Vs. Jagjit Works 1984 RLR 549. The Division bench observed as under:- Contd..... -12- ''it is useful to note that Section 85 raises a presumption about the execution of a POA provided two conditions are satisfied. Firstly it must be executed before a Notary Public and secondly it must be authenticated by him. In this case, there is no authentication at all. There is no statement of facts by the Notary public regarding the manner of execution or the persons executing the document. If reference is made to the judgements cited before us, the contrast is striking. In the case of the City Bank, the authentication made by the Notary Public in New York covers nearly two printed pages of the Report and quotes extensively the circumstances in which the General POA was executed. Similarly, in the case of the National and Grindlays Bank Ltd, the authentication shows that the seal of the bank was impressed on the POA in the presence of the Notary and the same was the genuine seal of the Bank. Thus it was the authentication that proved both the execution as well as the due authentication of POA and therefore, satisfied the test laid down in Sec. 85 of the Evidence Act.'' 15. In Susannee Lenatz Vs. Inernational Hotels Limited reported as 2005 (125) DLT 409: 2005 (85) DRJ 521 Hon'ble Mr. Justice Badar Durrez Ahmed Judge, Delhi High Court has held that under order VI Rule 14 of the Code of Civil Procedure 1908 every pleading shall be signed by the party Contd..... -13- and his pleader, if any. The proviso to the said Rule indicates that where a party pleading is, by reason of absence or for other good cause unable to sign the pleading it may be signed by any person duly authorised by him to sign the same or to sue or defend on his behalf. 16. In Syndicate Bank Vs. M/s S.A Trading Corpn & Ors 1990 (3) Delhi Lawyers 356, a Division Bench of Delhi High Court while dealing with the question of proof of power of attorney, where presumption under Section 85 of the Evidence Act could not be raised observed as under:- ''In case the person who has conferred the Power of Attorney has not got it executed, so as to enable him to raise the presumption which may be raised in terms of Section 85 of the Evidence Act, then he is left with no option, but to prove the same in accordance with law. This is done by proving the resolution of the Board of Directors of the company, which gives its officers power to grant Power of Attorney to persons the company considers worthy of it, and also prove the factual execution of the Power of Attorney by the empowered officer or officers. This proof has to be tendered in Court by proving the passing of the resolution by the company in accordance with sections 193 and 194 of the Companies Act, 1956.'' Contd..... -14- 17. Against judgment of dismissal of suit dated 11/11/2005, passed by Ld. Civil Judge, appellant/applicant had preferred the civil writ petition no.4251/06 & CM 3602/06, which had been dismissed as not maintainable, by Hon'ble Mr. Justice Pradeep Nandrajog, Judge, Delhi High Court vide order dated 22/3/2006. Order dated 22/3/2006 was passed in presence of Sh. Yog Verdhan, Advocate, appearing for present appellant/applicant. The following order was passed on 22/3/2006 :- 22/3/2006 Present : Mr. Yog Verdhan for the petitioner. Mr. Amit Mehra for Mr. Ajay Verma for DDA. +WP(C) No.4251/2006 & CM 3602/2006 On the same cause, in the year 1998, a suit was filed by the petitioner. The suit has suffered a dismissal on 11.11.2005. Decision of the civil court shows that notwithstanding it was held that the suit was barred by limitation, decision on merits has also been rendered. Petitioner has lost the battle even on merits. Decision of the learned Civil Judge shows that evidence was led. Based on evidence and whatever was produced, a reasoned decision has been taken. Contd..... -15- It is too late in the day for the petitioner to change tracks. A writ petition on the same cause is not maintainable today. Remedy of the petitioner is to challenge the decision of the learned Civil Judge by way of an appeal, should the petitioner feel aggrieved by the said decision . Petition dismissed as not maintainable. March 22, 2006 PRADEEP NANDRAJOG, J. 18. The order dated 22/3/2006 is very much clear, wherein Hon'ble Mr. Justice Pradeep Nandrajog, Judge, Delhi High Court, vide dismissing writ petition, filed by present appellant/applicant/plaintiff, has observed that the decision of Ld. Civil Judge shows that the evidence was led. Based on evidence and whatever was produced, a reasoned decision has been passed. It has also been observed in this order that it is too late for the petitioner Vivek Gupta, the present appellant to change tracks. It has further been mentioned in this order that remedy is to challenge the decision of Ld. Civil Judge by way of an appeal, should the petitioner feel aggrieved by the said decision . 19. In this background, the matter is being Contd..... -16- considered. All these applications have been contested by non-applicant/respondent/DDA. They have filed reply and applicant/appellant has filed rejoinder to these applications. These applications are being taken up in the following manner. 20. First of all, I shall take up the first application i.e. the application u/s 5 of Limitation Act for condonation of delay in filing plaint, dismissed on the ground of limitation. In this application the appellant/applicant has prayed for condonation of delay in filing the suit for mandatory injunction. 21. As earlier mentioned in preceding para, while deciding issue no.1 the Ld. Trial Judge has came to the conclusion that the relief prayed by appellant/applicant was time barred. Meaning thereby that the suit being time barred is one of the reasons for dismissing the suit. This reason of dismissing the suit on the point of limitation can be considered while deciding the regular appeal. It cannot be decided in piecemeal, by way of application u/s 5 of Limitation Act. 22. Even otherwise, Para 2 and 3 of this application mention that the shop was demolished on Contd..... -17- 26/3/1996 and notice u/s 80 CPC was sent on 04/11/1997, which was acknowledged by respondent on 10/1/1997, however, applicant counsel filed the suit on 13/7/1998 and did not move any application for condonation of delay as the suit should have been filed within six months from the date of mandatory notice. The averments made in this application clearly show that applicant/appellant himself admits that the relief prayed by him was time barred. Accordingly, this application u/s 5 of limitation Act stands dismissed. 23. Now I shall take up the second application i.e. u/o XLI R-3 A r/w Sec. 151 CPC for condonation of delay in filing the appeal. The impugned judgment was passed on 11/11/2005 and after that the present applicant/appellant had preferred civil writ petition civil no.4251/2006, which has been dismissed as not maintainable, vide order dated 22/3/2006 passed by Hon'ble Mr. Justice Pradeep Nandrajog, Judge, Delhi High Court. As per record the certified copy of order dated 22/3/2006 was applied on 23/3/2006, which was prepared on 29/3/2006 and delivered on 30/3/2006. It is pertinent to mention that even though certified copy of judgment dated 11/11/2005 passed by Ld. Civil Judge has been filed, but first page of title Contd..... -18- which mentions the particular of suit/first page of the plaint and on the reverse of the same regarding date of application, date of preparation of certified copy have been mentioned has not been filed for the reasons best known to the appellant/applicant. 24. In this application u/o XLI R-3A r/w Sec. 151 CPC, it has been submitted that the applicant had spent time in pursuing writ petition before Hon'ble High Court. As soon as applicant received information on 16/1/2006, from his attorney that the suit has been dismissed and acted on the advice of his counsel. The application u/o XLI R-3A r/w 151 CPC , runs into three paragraphs which read as under :- 1. ''That the applicant filed a suit on 13/7/98 for mandatory injunction for direction to the respondent to allot an alternative shop in lieu of his demolished shop at Muketeshwar Complex, Andheria Bagh, Mehrauli, New Delhi, which was dismissed by the Ld. Civil Judge, Sh. Ajay Pandey vide order dated 11/11/05. 2. That the applicant had spent time in pursuing a writ petition before Hon'ble Delhi High Court against the order of the Ld. Trial Court Judge Sh. Ajay Pandey, passed on 11/11/05 as soon as the applicant received information Contd..... -19- on 16/1/06 from his attorney that the suit has been dismissed and acted on the advice of his counsel therefore, the accompanying appeal has been delayed. 3. That in view of the above facts and circumstances it is humbly prayed that delay in filing the present appeal if any be condoned in the interest of justice.'' 25. In my considered view the applicant/appellant has failed to disclose cogent reasons to satisfy this court that there was sufficient cause for not preferring the appeal within the prescribed period. The suit was dismissed on 11/11/2005. As per the averments made in this application, his attorney had informed regarding dismissal only on 16/1/2006. Affidavit of attorney namely Sh. Sadashiv Gupta, who is real brother of present appellant/applicant, has not been filed for the reasons best known to the applicant. Even it has nowhere been disclosed as to the date when they had applied for obtaining certified copies and when did they collect the same. As earlier observed the first page of the plaint on the reverse of which it generally mention the details has not been filed. Contd..... -20- 26. The said writ petition was filed on 20/3/2006, which was dismissed vide order dated 22/3/2006, but the present appeal under disposal has been filed on 27/4/2006 i.e. after more than thirty five days from the date of dismissal of the writ petition. No reason has been explained in the application under disposal regarding non-filing of the appeal prior to 27/4/2006, after dismissal of writ petition on 22/3/2006. The delay at the most can be condoned for obtaining certified copy of impugned judgment dated 11/11/2005 and for obtaining copy of order dated 22/3/2006 vide which the writ petition was dismissed. 27. There is no explanation what to talk of any sufficient cause or cogent reasons for delay in any other respect including from the date of dismissal of suit on 11/11/2005 till filing of the present appeal except the delay in obtaining certified copy, even the period for filing writ cannot be condoned as it was not proper remedy opted for by the applicant/appellant. On the one hand attorney of the appellant was pursuing the matter it was he who had signed and verified the plaint and appellant/plaintiff was not examined even and now the averment has been that attorney Contd..... -21- had informed after more than two months regarding dismissal of the suit i.e. on 16/1/2006. In these circumstances, as there is no explanation regarding from period 11/11/2005 to 16/1/2006, there is no explanation in the application regarding the date when writ petition was filed and when it was dismissed and even observation of the Hon'ble High Court is for appeal but no liberty has been granted by the Hon'ble High Court, while dismissing the petition on 22/3/2006. No explanation has been given even after 22/3/2006 when writ petition was dismissed. There is no sufficient cause in filing the appeal after the expiry of period of limitation. Therefore, this application under disposal, is also devoid of merits. Delay in filing the appeal cannot be condoned and hence, the present application is dismissed. 28. The third application is u/o XLI R-23 r/w Sec. 107 & 151 CPC for remanding back the suit for fresh trial. Since the delay in filing the present appeal has not been condoned, the present application u/o XLI R-23 r/w Sec. 107 & 151 CPC, is also not maintainable. Order for remanding back the suit for fresh trial cannot be passed. Hence, the application u/o XLI R- 23 r/w Sec. 107 & 151 CPC stands dismissed. Contd..... -22- 29. The fourth application u/o XLI R-27 r/w Sec. 107 & 151 CPC is for production of additional evidence. In para 3 of this application, it has been mentioned that the applicant went into deep depression and the entire case was litigated by his brother/attorney and Ld. Civil Judge has rejected the evidence led by attorney, which was done as per the advice of then counsel. Since th application u/o XLI R-23 r/w Sec. 107 & 151 CPC, has been dismissed and delay in filing appeal has not been condoned, the appellant cannot be granted an opportunity for production of additional evidence. Hence, the present application u/o XLI R-27 r/w Sec. 107 & 151 CPC also stands dismissed. 30. In these circumstances, since admittedly the present appeal is time barred and the application u/o XLI R-3A r/w Sec.151 CPC has been dismissed, the main appeal also stands dismissed. Parties shall bear their own cost. Copy of this order be sent to Trial Court for information. Appeal file be consigned to Record Room. ANNOUNCED IN THE OPEN COURT ON 5th March, 2007. (PINKI) Additional District Judge, Delhi. Contd..... -23- RCA-21/06 05/3/2007 Present : Proxy counsel for parties. Vide my separate order announced today, the four pending applications, moved by appellant/applicant are dismissed. The main appeal also stands dismissed. Parties shall bear their own cost. Copy of this order be sent to Trial Court for information. Appeal file be consigned to Record Room. (PINKI) ADDITIONAL DISTRICT JUDGE DELHI/05.03.2007 Contd.....
